beat.text: The size of the numbers don't matter. Think about the arrays themselves. When you look at the rows versus the columns, do any dots disappear or appear?

thread.comment_text: All of these remediations used “flip an array” but they haven’t yet flipped anything, that comes up next
I made edits to change the language away from “flip an array”, but I wanted you to be aware @Kesha Bagadia. I don’t know how we limit remediations to what they have already been taught
Interesting. Do not resolve this, I will investigate

issue_summary: Remediation dialogue references 'flipping an array' when students have not yet learned that concept in the lesson sequence.
reasoning: The beat is inside validator[1] (incorrect[1]), making it remediation dialogue generated by remediation_generator. The issue is that the remediation assumes knowledge ('flip an array') that students haven't encountered yet in the lesson flow. The remediation_generator is responsible for calibrating difficulty and ensuring remediation hints align with what students have already learned.
suggested_fix: Regenerate remediation dialogue to reference only concepts already introduced (rows, columns, reading arrays differently) without using the term 'flip an array'.

beat.text: Let me show you. Watch it rotate: the 5 rows of 3 become 3 rows of 5. Count them. 3 rows. Each row has 5 dots. That's 3 times 5. And it still has the same total of 15 dots after turning it.

thread.comment_text: I don’t think we need both of these (it popped up in a few other spots and I deleted one of the versions). The guide will say them exactly the same so it will be repetitive. I’m thinking it will be how she says it with “times” and not the equation, but I don’t actually care which one stays.

I think this is an interesting note because we could benefit from clarifying that the script is meant to be listened to more than seen. It does afford additional visual affordance but we rely on prompts and workspace to communicate visually.

issue_summary: Remediation dialogue contains redundant phrasing: both '3 times 5' and '3 × 5' express the same concept, creating repetition when spoken aloud.
reasoning: The beat is inside validator[4] (incorrect[4]), making it remediation dialogue generated by remediation_generator. The issue is content-level redundancy in the Heavy modeling response, not a tone or voice violation. The dialogue says both 'That's 3 times 5' and later references the equation, which the reviewer notes will sound repetitive when spoken.
suggested_fix: Remove either the spoken form ('3 times 5') or the equation reference to eliminate redundancy in the remediation dialogue.

params.description: Array rotates. Now 5 rows of 8 dots. Expression updates to 5 × 8 = 40.

thread.comment_text: Only thing to mention just as a risk /downside of the decision we made (but I don’t feel it outweighs the pros!) - the starter pack had an error. The script accurately changed the animation and equation to be correct like they should be (which wasn’t aligned with the SP), but because we are maintaining the language from the SP, this on correct line was still incorrect. The lack of alignment was a flag for me to check what what happening, but it was a true error in the SP that was carried through in NOT changing the guide dialogue
Interesting. I can let the AI flag things for us to fix even if I don’t empower it to edit things itself

beat.text: After rotation, will the product be the same or different?

validator.description: Student selected Same, correct, internalized that rotation preserves product

params.description: Array rotates. Now 5 rows of 8 dots. Expression updates to 5 × 8 = 40.

beats.text: That's it. 8 times 5. Still 40. Same dots, just turned.

validator.description: Student selected Different, lost rows, focused on row count instead of total

params.description: All dots pulse.

beats.text: Here's what helps. Rotation moves dots around, but no dots disappear. Count all the dots. How many are there?

validator.description: Student selected Different, lost columns, focused on column count instead of total

params.description: All dots pulse.

beats.text: You're working on it. Turning the array doesn't add or remove dots. We still have all 40 dots. Do they go away when we rotate?

validator.description: Student selected Different, factors changed, confused changing order with changing product

params.description: All dots pulse.

beats.text: Let's think about this a bit more. The factors do swap their positions, but the total number of dots stays the same. 8 times 5 equals what?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Array rotates. Now 5 rows of 8 dots. Expression updates to 5 × 8 = 40.

beats.text: Here, let me walk you through this one. Right now, we have 8 times 5, which equals 40 dots. When I rotate the array, the factors swap. We get 5 times 8. But 5 times 8 also equals 40. Same dots, same total. The product stays 40.

issue_summary: The on_correct dialogue says '5 times 8' but the starter pack incorrectly said '8 times 5', and this error was preserved in the dialogue despite the scene animation being corrected to show 5×8.
reasoning: The section_structurer generates initial on_correct dialogue content from the starter pack. The reviewer confirms the starter pack had an error (said '8 times 5' when it should say '5 times 8' after rotation). The scene beat was correctly generated (shows 5 rows of 8 dots, expression 5×8=40), but the dialogue beat preserved the starter pack's incorrect wording. The dialogue_rewriter only enforces tone/style rules, not factual accuracy — it would not catch or fix a mathematical error like stating the wrong factor order.
suggested_fix: Correct the on_correct dialogue to say '5 times 8' to match the rotated array configuration shown in the scene.

beat.text: You picked 6 rows of 4. That makes 24 dots, but it's not a rotation of 3 rows of 8. When you rotate an array, the rows become columns and the columns become rows. Find which array shows 3 rows of 8 rotated.

thread.comment_text: I wanted to leave these suggestions as a sample. Sometimes I think remediations are missing the call to action after she remediates. Restating what they need to do. Posing another question. Giving some action instead of just statements about the content they missed. I feel like in sections I added a lot of these which I’m thinking will help the flow.
Good note! Keep this unresolved. I will resolve once it is fixed.
I resolved the suggestions to let us ingest and move forward in the meantime.

beat.text: Which array is the same as 3 × 8, just rotated?

validator.description: Student selected B) 8 × 3, correct. Rotation swaps 3 rows of 8 into 8 rows of 3.

params.description: Option B highlights to confirm selection.

beats.text: Right. 8 rows of 3. That's 3 rows of 8, just turned. Same dots, same product. Now look at the others: 4 × 6, 6 × 4, 2 × 12. They all have 24 dots too, but they're different shapes. You can't rotate 3 rows of 8 and get 4 rows of 6. Same product doesn't mean same array.

validator.description: Student selected A) 4 × 6. Same product (24) but different structure than 3 × 8.

params.description: Option A highlights.

beats.text: You picked 4 rows of 6. That makes 24 dots, but it's not the same array turned. Look at the reference: 3 rows of 8. If you rotate that, how many rows would you get?

validator.description: Student selected C) 6 × 4. Same product (24) but different structure than 3 × 8.

params.description: Option C highlights.

issue_summary: Remediation dialogue ends with explanatory statements but lacks a clear call to action directing the student what to do next.
reasoning: The beat is inside validator[2] (incorrect[2]), making it remediation dialogue generated by remediation_generator. The reviewer notes that remediation lacks actionable next steps after explaining the error — it tells the student what's wrong but doesn't restate the task or pose a follow-up question. This is a structural issue with how remediation_generator frames incorrect-attempt feedback.
suggested_fix: Add a clear call to action at the end of remediation dialogue, such as restating the prompt or posing a guiding question to direct the student's next attempt.
